sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Click (46, 40) changed the bottom-right grid's middle-left cell from blue to red. The mechanic is clicking on cells changes their color. The bottom-right grid has a charcoal frame, suggesting it's the target/template. All other grids have red cells that need to match the all-blue bottom-right grid.
Goal model: Need to turn all red cells in the three non-framed grids to blue, and fix the accidental red cell in the bottom-right grid.
Plan: First test if clicking toggles color (red->blue), then systematically turn all red cells to blue.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First, let me click on (46, 40) again to see if it toggles back to blue
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 40}])
result = last_action_result
print(f"Click (46, 40) again: board_changed={result.get('board_changed', False)}")

# Check what changed
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Row 46, cols 36-43: '{ascii_lines[46][36:44]}'")

# Now let me identify ALL red cells in all grids
# Top-left grid: rows 2-23, cols 4-21
# Top-right grid: rows 2-23, cols 32-49
# Bottom-left grid: rows 36-57, cols 4-21
# Bottom-right grid: rows 36-57, cols 36-53

# Find all red 6x6 cells using segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
red_cells = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'R' and node['pixels'] == 36:
        boundary = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        red_cells.append((min_r, min_c, max_r, max_c, node['id']))

print(f"\nRed 6x6 cells found: {len(red_cells)}")
for rc in red_cells:
    print(f"  rows {rc[0]}-{rc[2]}, cols {rc[1]}-{rc[3]}")

# Also find blue 6x6 cells
blue_cells = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'b' and node['pixels'] == 36:
        boundary = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        blue_cells.append((min_r, min_c, max_r, max_c, node['id']))

print(f"\nBlue 6x6 cells found: {len(blue_cells)}")
for bc in blue_cells:
    print(f"  rows {bc[0]}-{bc[2]}, cols {bc[1]}-{bc[3]}")

# The goal is likely to make all grids match the bottom-right pattern (all blue)
# So I need to click on all red cells to turn them blue
# Click center of each red cell
print(f"\nClicking on {len(red_cells)} red cells to turn them blue:")
for rc in red_cells:
    center_r = (rc[0] + rc[2]) // 2
    center_c = (rc[1] + rc[3]) // 2
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_r, 'col': center_c}])
    r = last_action_result
    changed = r.get('board_changed', False)
    print(f"  Click ({center_r}, {center_c}): board_changed={changed}")
    if r.get('done') or r.get('level_completed'):
        print("  *** LEVEL COMPLETED! ***")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Click (46, 40) changed the bottom-right grid's middle-left cell from blue to red. The mechanic is clicking on cells changes their color. The bottom-right grid has a charcoal frame, suggesting it's the target/template. All other grids have red cells that need to match the all-blue bottom-right grid.
Goal model: Need to turn all red cells in the three non-framed grids to blue, and fix the accidental red cell in the bottom-right grid.
Plan: First test if clicking toggles color (red->blue), then systematically turn all red cells to blue.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First, let me click on (46, 40) again to see if it toggles back to blue
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 40}])
result = last_action_result
print(f"Click (46, 40) again: board_changed={result.get('board_changed', False)}")

# Check what changed
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Row 46, cols 36-43: '{ascii_lines[46][36:44]}'")

# Now let me identify ALL red cells in all grids
# Top-left grid: rows 2-23, cols 4-21
# Top-right grid: rows 2-23, cols 32-49
# Bottom-left grid: rows 36-57, cols 4-21
# Bottom-right grid: rows 36-57, cols 36-53

# Find all red 6x6 cells using segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
red_cells = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'R' and node['pixels'] == 36:
        boundary = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        red_cells.append((min_r, min_c, max_r, max_c, node['id']))

print(f"\nRed 6x6 cells found: {len(red_cells)}")
for rc in red_cells:
    print(f"  rows {rc[0]}-{rc[2]}, cols {rc[1]}-{rc[3]}")

# Also find blue 6x6 cells
blue_cells = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'b' and node['pixels'] == 36:
        boundary = node['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        blue_cells.append((min_r, min_c, max_r, max_c, node['id']))

print(f"\nBlue 6x6 cells found: {len(blue_cells)}")
for bc in blue_cells:
    print(f"  rows {bc[0]}-{bc[2]}, cols {bc[1]}-{bc[3]}")

# The goal is likely to make all grids match the bottom-right pattern (all blue)
# So I need to click on all red cells to turn them blue
# Click center of each red cell
print(f"\nClicking on {len(red_cells)} red cells to turn them blue:")
for rc in red_cells:
    center_r = (rc[0] + rc[2]) // 2
    center_c = (rc[1] + rc[3]) // 2
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_r, 'col': center_c}])
    r = last_action_result
    changed = r.get('board_changed', False)
    print(f"  Click ({center_r}, {center_c}): board_changed={changed}")
    if r.get('done') or r.get('level_completed'):
        print("  *** LEVEL COMPLETED! ***")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
